retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Tissue-Based Biomarkers -- Diagnostic Tests -- Tissue-Based Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Multigene molecular testing for patients with low, intermediate, and high-risk prostate cancer who enjoy an estimated life expectancy ≥10 years is suggested by the NCCN expert committee. The most recent NCCN guidelines recommend tumor genetic testing for homologous recombination repair mutations, microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H), deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) genes, and tumor mutational burden in patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Homologous recombination repair (HRR) genes: The HRR pathway is a metabolic process that aids the homology-mediated repair of damaged DNA. [55] The genes included in the testing panel for metastatic prostate cancer, as suggested by the NCCN expert committee, are BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, BARD1, BRIP1, CDK12, CHEK1, CHEK2, FANCL, PALB2, RAD51B, RAD51C, RAD51D, and RAD54L. It may be suitable for a PARP inhibitor. (see below)

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Tissue-Based Biomarkers -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Deletions in APC, CHD1, ETS2, NKX3.1, and SETD2. Deletions/mutations in ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, ERF, KMT2A, KMT2C, KMT2D, KDM1A, KDM3A, KDM6A, NCOR1, NCOR2, PTEN, RB1, SMAD4, SMARCA1, SMARCB1, and TP53. Amplification/mutation/splice variant mutations in AR. Fusion/deletion in ERG and  ETVs. Mutations in EZH2, FOXA1, IDH1, and SPOP. Amplification in MYC, MYCN, and SETDB1 .

retrieved_docs.title: Biomarker Assays for Elevated Prostate-Specific Antigen Risk Analysis -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genomic evaluation has identified more than 100 genes contributing to the risk of prostatic malignancy. [5] [6] [7] About 15% to 17% of the prostate cancer population have been identified as having germline mutations. Such mutations are independent of the cancer stage. A study of multigene testing among men with prostate cancer across the United States by Giri et al concluded that 15.6% of the study population had pathogenic variations of the genes studied, including BRCA1 , BRCA2 , HOXB13 , MLH1 , MSH2 , PMS2 , MSH6 , EPCAM , ATM CHEK2 , NBN , and TP53 . [8] [9] The most commonly found pathologic gene variation identified was BRCA2 (4.5%). [8] Nicolosi et al reported that 17.2% of the study population had a pathogenic germline variant, and genomic analysis could help identify high-risk families. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Germline Testing in Prostate Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations have been found in up to 11.8% of men with metastatic prostate cancer and 5% to 7% of patients with localized disease. [397] [398] BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutation status is an independent prognostic indicator of metastasis-free survival in patients with localized disease undergoing definitive therapy (either radiation or radical prostatectomy). [402] [403] Men with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations are also at higher risk for male breast cancer, melanoma, and pancreatic cancer. [397] [398] Men with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations face an overall increased risk of prostate cancer (3.8-fold higher risk for BRCA1 and 8.6-fold higher risk for BRCA2) and are more likely to present with advanced disease, a higher Gleason Score, and tend to have shorter cancer-specific survival than noncarriers. [403] [404] These men also have worse outcomes after radical prostatectomy or radiation therapy. [403]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Etiology -- Genetics
retrieved_docs.content: Over 100 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and other genes have been linked to an increased risk of prostate cancer, including hereditary prostate cancer gene 1, various androgen and Vitamin D receptor genes, HPC1, HPC2, HPCX, CAPB, mutL homolog 1 (MLH1), mutS homologs 2 and 6 (MSH2 and MSH6), postmeiotic segregation increased 2 (PMS2), homeobox B13 (HOXB13), checkpoint kinase 2 (CHEK2), nibrin (NBN), BRCA1-interacting protein C-terminal helicase 1 (BRIP1), ataxia telangiectasia mutated (ATM), and the TMPRSS2-ETS gene family, such as TMPRSS2-ERG and TMPRSS2-ETV1/4 , all of which tend to promote cancer cell growth. [26] [34] [36] (Note: This is only a partial listing. Clinically significant germline mutations are discussed in subsequent sections.)

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Summary of Prostate Cancer Systemic Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: All protocols begin with hormonal therapy using an LHRH agonist or antagonist. Due to improved outcomes and survival, a second-generation antiandrogen should be added to ADT in the upfront setting for the majority of patients. In advanced or aggressive cases, triplet therapy with docetaxel and a second-generation antiandrogen should be used. Docetaxel is the recommended first-line chemotherapy. Cabazitaxel is a second-line chemotherapy drug. Although similar in efficacy to docetaxel, cabazitaxel  is better tolerated and preferred in patients at risk for neutropenia, extremely frail patients, or older patients. However, it is more expensive compared to docetaxel. Patients with ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, Fanconi anemia complementation group A (FANCA), and PALB2 germline mutations that involve DDRGs tend to have an unusual sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy and are likely to respond to PARP inhibitors such as olaparib and rucaparib.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Specific Germline Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: Some experts have suggested testing for all the HRR mutations, including ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, BARD1, BRIP1, CDK12, CHEK1, CHEK2, FANCL, PALB2, RAD51B, RAD51C, RAD51D, and RAD54L; the MMR mutations, including PMS2, MLH1, MSH2, and MSH6; EpCAM; HOXB13, especially in African Americans; FANCA; NBN; KLK3|179T; and p53. This decision to pursue such testing depends on commercial availability, cost, insurance coverage, potential usability of the resulting information, availability of quality genetic counseling, and the overall reliability of the testing provided. Specific genetic testing platforms test for the aforementioned genes and additional genes as part of their panels. [453] If genetic counseling services are limited in your area, contact the National Society of Genetic Counselors for assistance and a directory of their members. Genetic counseling services are also available online.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Systemic Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitors are a type of enzyme that helps repair DNA damage in cells. PARP inhibitors, such as olaparib, rucaparib, talazaparib, and niraparib, prevent cancer cells from repairing DNA damage, which facilitates apoptosis. They are considered a type of targeted therapy as they work best in patients with DNA damage repair gene (DDRG) germline or somatic mutations. Olaparib showed a median survival benefit of about 5 months (more than double the median PFS) compared to enzalutamide or abiraterone treatment alone and was most effective in patients with BRCA2 mutations on germline testing. [350] Rucaparib demonstrated a 63% PSA response rate. [369] Patients with PALB2, BRIP1, and RAD51B mutations responded quite well to rucaparib therapy, whereas those with ATM, cyclin-dependent kinase 12 (CDK12), and CHEK1 germline mutations were generally refractory to the drug. [349] [370] PARP inhibitors have demonstrated the ability to extend OS and make prostate cancer more radiosensitive in early clinical trials. [349] [350] [351] [352] [353] [369] [371] [372] This radiosensitizing effect could further increase their efficacy as more research is conducted in this area. [371]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the proper use of newly available genomic tests and determining which are optimal at different stages of evaluation

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who fail treatment with second-generation antiandrogens and chemotherapy may be candidates for PARP inhibitors. Germline and somatic testing, especially BRCA1/2, should be performed to assess for DDRGs. Also, consider obtaining an AR-V7 blood test. Patients with BRCA1 and BRCA 2 mutations respond better to PARP inhibitors compared to those with ATM or other mutations. [376] Patients with MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2 that involve DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes may respond to an immunotherapy drug such as pembrolizumab, although this is a small group of patients. [377] Patients with castrate-resistant prostate cancer and bone metastases benefit from either zoledronic acid or denosumab to minimize bone pain and fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Tissue-Based Biomarkers -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The genome and epigenome play a crucial role in the initiation and progression of prostatic malignancy. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have shown that about 31.5% of the familial relative risk (FRR) is attributed to 167 common variants or susceptible loci in the genome, and approximately 6% are due to BRCA2 and HOXB13 . The first GWAS (2006) found single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the 8q24 loci, which are believed to influence the oncogene MYC . Besides HOXB13 and BRCA2 , familial mutations have also been found in ATM , CHEK2 , BRCA1 , RAD51D , and PALB2 genes. [20] As for common somatic variants found in prostatic cancer, the following are implicated: [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Specific Germline Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: The NCCN recommends at least 11 mutations for germline testing in prostate cancer—ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDK12, CHEK2, PALB2, RAD51D, PMS2, MLH1, MSH2, and MSH6. Some experts have recommended adding EpCAM; HOXB13, especially in African Americans; FANCA; P53, and NBN. Germline mutations are mostly of 2 general types—DDRGs and DNA MMR genes.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Specific Germline Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: DDRGs include ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, CDK12, FANCA, RAD51D, and PALB2. This group often responds to PARP inhibitor therapy, although the benefit is largely driven by BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations. Patients with DDRG mutations also appear to respond to cisplatin, which is currently being investigated and has promising efficacy, but with a significant toxicity profile. [417] [418] The combination of PARP inhibition and radiation therapy may also prove useful in patients with this type of mutation. [419] There are 276 known DDRG germline mutations. African Americans with prostate cancer appear to have relatively high numbers of RAD51, RAD54B, RAD54L, PMS2, and BRCA1 mutations compared to non-African American prostate cancer patients. [416] MMR mutations include MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2. Patients with these genetic defects tend to respond to immunotherapy such as pembrolizumab, although it is of low prevalence in prostate cancer compared to other malignancies. [420] [421]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Summary of Genomic Prostate Cancer Tests (Beyond Prostate-Specific Antigen)
retrieved_docs.content: Post-biopsy: For  patients with a negative initial tissue biopsy who are being considered for a repeat prostatic biopsy, tissue-based genomic bioassays such as ConfirmMDx provide valuable risk stratification and analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Germline Testing in Prostate Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Germline mutations were found in 43% of younger prostate cancer patients (<55 years) compared to only 9% in men over 85 years with similar clinical disease. [405] Higher Gleason scores have been associated with BRCA2, ATM, and NBN mutations. [406] The NCCN recommends that men with a BRCA2 mutation should start annual prostate cancer screenings at age 40. A cutoff of 3 ng/mL has been suggested for this group. The same is suggested for men with BRCA1 mutations. [398] [407]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Curiously, African American men have shown a relatively low incidence of germline mutations despite their well-known genetic predisposition to aggressive prostate cancer compared to the general population. However, a higher incidence of RAD family mutations (RAD51, RAD54L, RAD54B), and PMS2 and BRCA1 in African Americans (but not White race individuals in the US), might explain this difference and offer an opportunity for better risk-stratification and opportunities for research into targeted therapies for this high-risk group. [415] [416]

Melanomas of skin have been reported with a finding site in the retina, highlighting possible ocular involvement. Collectively, these hierarchical and inverse‑is‑a relationships create an integrated network that connects hereditary syndromes, primary organ‑specific cancers, and diverse metastatic presentations to the central concept of malignant tumors and secondary malignancies, supporting precise diagnosis, classification, and management of oncologic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are noted to co‑occur with حصاة حالبية and also with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, indicating that patients who present with vague or nonspecific genitourinary complaints may simultaneously have ureteral stones (حصاة حالبية) or prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing this association can prompt clinicians to consider targeted diagnostic evaluations for these specific urologic pathologies when faced with ambiguous genitourinary presentations, thereby improving detection and management of underlying stone disease or malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) is a key anatomical site within the male genital system and is the focus of specific diagnostic procedures that are applied when the prostate tissue has been partially excised. This organ serves as the anatomic location for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), linking the disease directly to the prostate tissue. In addition, the prostate is identified as the location of a broader category of prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي), which functions as a parent classification for other male genital disorders. These other male genital disorders are themselves situated in the prostate and are closely associated with screening activities (التحصّي). Together, these relationships highlight the central role of the prostate in both disease manifestation and diagnostic evaluation, emphasizing the importance of targeted procedures and screening in the management of prostate‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions arise within the urinary system and frequently co‑occur with prostatic disorders, seminal calculi, and both localized and metastatic prostate cancer; they are also clinically associated with prostate cancer. The prostate is an organ that belongs to the urinary system and serves as the anatomic site for prostate‑related diseases, including metastatic prostate cancer. Prostatic disorders are situated in the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise located in the prostate, representing subcategories of prostatic disorders. These other male genital disorders are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships emphasize that disturbances of the prostate and associated genitourinary symptoms are central to the presentation, co‑occurrence, and clinical management of prostate disease, particularly cancer, and underscore the importance of appropriate diagnostic screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2262
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (ureteral stone), سرطـان البروستاتا النَّقيلي (metastatic prostate cancer), and فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia). This pattern of co‑occurrence indicates that patients who present with nonspecific genitourinary complaints may also have underlying stone disease, advanced prostate malignancy, or benign prostatic enlargement, underscoring the need for thorough diagnostic assessment to identify and manage these potentially related conditions.

General prostatic disorders (اضطراب بروستاتي) are placed under “other male genital disorders,” associated with screening/preventive measures (التحصّي) and connected to the prostate through a location‑of relationship. Collectively, these relationships provide a structured framework that maps biopsy and other diagnostic methods to specific organ systems, classifies therapeutic procedures within gender‑specific categories, and integrates oncologic entities such as malignant prostate tumors and their patterns of spread, thereby supporting comprehensive clinical decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Other male genital disorders are considered a subcategory of prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and are linked to the practice of screening (التحصّي). Recognizing that other male genital disorders fall under the broader category of prostate disorder and are associated with screening emphasizes the importance of appropriate diagnostic evaluation in the management of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 138
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is associated with an increased risk of pancreatic cancer, specifically carcinoma of the head of the pancreas, which is described as a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني). Pancreatic cancer and other non‑hematologic malignant diseases such as prostate glandular cancer, head‑and‑neck squamous cell carcinoma, lung cancer of non‑smoking‑related cells, and cancer of the trachea (classified under “cancer; other respiratory and intrathoracic”) are grouped within the SMQ “الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة”. Within this framework, metastatic malignant neoplasms to the head and neck are organized hierarchically: the broad category “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head” includes “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the head,” which together with “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck” are classified as secondary malignancies. These entities are also listed as children of the clinical classification “NEOPLASMS OF THE MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM AND SOFT

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1885
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions represent a broad clinical category that includes specific bladder disorders (اضطراب في المثانة). These nonspecific genitourinary presentations frequently co‑occur with renal calculi (حصاة حالبية) and are clinically associated with prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا). Recognizing that bladder disorders fall under this umbrella, and that patients presenting with such symptoms may also have urinary stones or underlying prostate malignancy, helps clinicians pursue appropriate diagnostic evaluations and tailor management strategies for comprehensive urologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1913
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE GENITOURINARY SYSTEM provides a hierarchical framework in which cancer of prostate is positioned as a subcategory. Within the cancer of prostate category, the specific malignant prostate tumor grouping identified as أورام البروستاتا الخبيثة (SMQ) is included as a member. This structured classification helps organize prostate neoplasms for accurate diagnosis, research, and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1106
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) serves as the anatomic site for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). A prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) is located within the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise situated in the prostate. These other male genital disorders are classified as a subcategory of the prostatic disorder (they “have parent” اضطراب بروستاتي) and are associated with screening or preventive measures (التحصّي). Recognizing that the prostate is the primary location for both metastatic prostate cancer and various male genital disorders, and that these disorders are linked to screening activities, underscores the importance of targeted prostate evaluation and appropriate preventive strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Prostata is identified as the anatomic site associated with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, indicating that prostate adenocarcinoma originates in the prostate. In addition, پروستاتا is noted as the location of اضطراب بروستاتي, reflecting that various prostatic disorders occur in this organ. Recognizing the prostate as the common site for both malignant and non‑malignant conditions highlights its clinical significance for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.
